front: Composer: Russian; member of the Mighty Five; motto 'to new shores'; died of alcohol-related illness at 42
Composer: The Nursery song cycle inspired Debussy's Children's Corner
Composer: Marriage — unfinished 'experiment in dramatic music in prose'; organized syllables to mimic conversational speech; influenced by Dargomyzhsky's The Stone Guest
Suite: piano suite inspired by Viktor Hartmann drawings; orchestrated by Maurice Ravel; opening Promenade alternates 5/4 and 6/4 time
Suite: in Ravel's orchestration, alto saxophone plays 'The Old Castle'; tuba plays 'Bydlo' (the ox-cart); piccolo and high strings depict 'Ballet of Chicks in Their Shells'
Suite: 'Hut on Fowl's Legs' opens with a dissonant falling major seventh from F-sharp to G; inspired by Hartmann's clock drawing; suite ends with 'The Great Gate of Kiev'
Suite: Vladimir Stasov provided annotations; Henry Wood orchestrated it in 1915 but deferred to Ravel; Stokowski removed 'Tuileries' and 'Limoges' from his orchestration
Tone Poem: depicts a witches' sabbath on St. John's Eve (worship of Chernobog); assembled posthumously by Rimsky-Korsakov; spirits disperse when a distant church bell rings
Tone Poem: made famous by Disney's Fantasia (1940); Stokowski's arrangement removed the 'fanfare' depicting the dark god

Opera: depicts Tsar Boris Godunov during the Time of Troubles; only complete opera by the composer; Coronation Scene has 38 bars of alternating A-flat and D dominant seventh chords (bells) and 'Slava' chorus
Opera: Act II ends with tritone ostinato (A–E-flat) as Boris hallucinates the bloody body of the murdered Tsarevich; clock chimes
Opera: rejected for lacking female leads; revised to add the 'Polish Act' and expand roles of Marina and Xenia; 'Farewell' scene is the most celebrated aria in Russian opera
Opera: Feodor Chaliapin was particularly famous in the title role; also performed by Mark Reizen and Boris Christoff; standard version edited by Rimsky-Korsakov
Opera: unfinished; depicts Streltsy rebellion against Peter the Great; Old Believers commit suicide by immolation; features 'Dance of the Persian Slaves' and a dawn Prelude
Song Cycle: four songs partly sung by Death; based on Golenishchev-Kutuzov poems; includes 'Lullaby,' 'Trepak,' 'Serenade,' 'The Field Marshal'
Song Cycle: 'The Field Marshal' — Death boasts of trampling mass graves; piano borrows from Polish chorale 'With the smoke of fires'; Shostakovich orchestrated it for Galina Vishnevskaya
